main_summary.summary: Gloria Arellanes, a Tongva elder born in 1946 and lifelong resident of El Monte, California, recounts her family history, her path to Chicano-movement activism, and her founding role in the Brown Berets. She describes her parents' purchase of an El Monte home on GI benefits in the late 1940s, the racial covenants the sellers tried to invoke against her Tongva mother, and her father's wrought-iron shop in East Los Angeles. She recalls identity confusion between American, Chicano, and Tongva framings, racism and race riots at El Monte High School, and dialogues with Anglo surfer students facilitated by Counselor Jack Barton. Her first job came through a youth employment project; she then worked at the Neighborhood Adult Participation Project (NAPP) in East Los Angeles. In the mid-1960s, friends took her to La Piranya Coffee House on Olympic Boulevard, where she met the young people who would become the Brown Berets under David Sanchez. She describes the Brown Berets' opening of an East Los Angeles free clinic with Physicians for Community Action and Psychologists for Community Action, the Watts-rebellion non-aggression coalition pact with Black organizations, and ties to US Organization, the Black Panthers, UMAS, Lucha, La Junta, and the Ex-Pintos.
key_themes: Tongva identity and Indigenous California history
Brown Berets founding and East Los Angeles Chicano movement
main_summary.historical_significance: Arellanes is one of the founding women of the Brown Berets and a Tongva elder, and her testimony bridges two historical narratives that are rarely held together: the Spanish-mission-era and 19th-century erasure of the Gabrielino-Tongva people of the Los Angeles basin, and the late-1960s Chicano movement that organized out of East Los Angeles. She names La Piranya Coffee House on Olympic Boulevard as the birthplace of the Brown Berets and describes the sheriff harassment that closed it. She documents the founding of the East Los Angeles free clinic as a coalition between the Brown Berets, Physicians for Community Action, and Psychologists for Community Action, with David Sanchez as prime minister. She also records the Watts-rebellion-era non-aggression pact between Chicano and Black organizations, and ongoing relationships with US Organization, the Black Panthers, UMAS, Lucha, La Junta, and the Ex-Pintos. As a primary source the interview gives first-person documentation of a Brown Berets founding member who is also a recognized Tongva elder, joining two California civil-rights lineages within one biography.

chapters.part: A Tongva Daughter in El Monte
chapters.title: The Place of the Willow
chapters.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in El Monte, California, on June 26, 2016, with Guha Shankar and John Bishop present, Gloria Arellanes introduces herself as born in 1946 and a lifelong resident, and identifies herself as a Tongva elder whose ancestors held this land, the village of Houtgna, the place of the Willow, before development erased what was natural there.

chapters.part: A Tongva Daughter in El Monte
chapters.title: We Have to Take This House Back
chapters.summary: Arellanes recounts how her fair-skinned father bought this El Monte house on his GI benefits, and how the sellers, having dealt only with him, tried to rescind the sale on the closing day when her dark, native-looking Tongva mother arrived to sign, until her father threatened to sue and kept the home, a refusal she learned decades later stemmed from rules barring sales to Mexicans and Asians.

chapters.part: A Tongva Daughter in El Monte
chapters.title: No, Dad, I'm American
chapters.summary: Arellanes describes the racism she met in an Anglo El Monte, the high school race riots where only Chicanos were arrested and students were mocked for their bean burritos, and the confusion of a father who insisted she was Chicano against a mother who, shown a cousin's genealogy of Indian vaqueros, told her never to call herself Indian and to say only that she was Mexican.

chapters.part: A Tongva Daughter in El Monte
chapters.title: History Said We No Longer Exist
chapters.summary: Arellanes explains the cultural trauma her mother carried, how the Spaniards forced Tongva women and children into the missions, stripped away their language, dress, dances, and ceremonies, and how mass disease left thousands buried in unmarked graves, and counters the claim that the people went extinct by describing a growing tribe reclaiming regalia and recovering language from John Peabody Harrington's recordings.

chapters.part: A Conservative Father and a Fighter's Youth
chapters.title: He Wouldn't Let Us Speak Spanish
chapters.topic: Her Father and the Musician Family
chapters.summary: Arellanes contrasts her conservative, hard-to-reach father, a businessman and Mason who clashed with her ideologically yet labeled her Chicano, with her opposite, best-friend mother, and recalls his refusal to let his children speak Spanish for fear of an accent, alongside his family of traveling musicians from Chihuahua and the pipe organ in her grandmother's East LA house that she would sneak in to play.

chapters.part: A Conservative Father and a Fighter's Youth
chapters.title: It Just Makes You a Fighter
chapters.topic: High School Organizing and Becoming Tough
chapters.summary: Arellanes traces her first activism to high school, where racism drew students together for protection, a parent offered her home for a youth council, and counselor Jack Barton let them hold meetings and defuse the race riots by talking with the surfers and reaching younger kids at the junior highs, and she describes how her size, near three hundred pounds at five foot eight, and years of bullying made her a fighter ready for the movement.

chapters.title: A Coffee House in the Dark
chapters.summary: After the recording resumes, Arellanes lists her early jobs, a youth employment project, a satin-ornament factory, and the East LA anti-poverty program NAPP, and tells how, while cruising Whittier Boulevard, she was invited to the La Piranya Coffee House on Olympic Boulevard, where people sat in the dark as a sheriff's floodlights swept the windows, a young community action group then forming the Brown Berets.

chapters.title: The Birthplace of the Chicano Movement
chapters.summary: Arellanes describes the passions she developed hearing the Brown Berets speak of police brutality, barred college access, poor jobs, bad housing, and unmet health care, her decision to join after three visits and leave East LA College for life experience, her view of La Piranya as the birthplace of the Chicano movement, and the training to never argue, recalling a man who crumpled her flyer and threw it at her feet.

chapters.title: They Welcomed Us With Open Arms
chapters.topic: Allied Movements and the Free Clinic
chapters.summary: Arellanes recalls watching other movements and groups form, UMAS in the colleges and outfits like Lucha and La Junta, the infighting she tried to rise above by getting along with everyone, and the cross-racial ties of the Brown Berets, attending the US Organization's Sunday gatherings of Swahili and regalia, links to the Black Panthers, a Watts-era non-aggression pact, and the free clinic David Sanchez opened with Anglo and Jewish physicians for community action.

loc_video.caption: Gloria Arellanes oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in El Monte, California, 2016 June 26
